clean_story:
Concrete runways. Steel birds nesting briefly.

The word airport merges "air" (the sky) and "port" (a docking place)—like a harbor, but for planes instead of ships. Yet unlike ships, planes can’t float while waiting; they must balance on wheels, burning fuel just to stand still.

An airport is a choreographed chaos. Luggage rides 10-mile underground rivers on conveyor belts, faster than Olympic sprinters. A single Boeing 747’s tires touch down at 160 miles per hour, then stop in under a minute—like slamming a bicycle’s brakes from a highway speed. The tarmac’s painted lines aren’t decoration: a misaligned 737 could clip wings with another plane, so pilots follow them like surgeons tracing veins.

At Dubai International, the world’s busiest hub, 240,000 meals are loaded daily—each tray timed to land warm. In Barrow, Alaska, the runway is gravel; in Gibraltar, it crosses a public road, halted by traffic lights. Every airport solves the same problem differently: how to pause the sky.

Three related subjects are floating airports on water, how air traffic controllers work, and why some planes never land.
